Continue your revision with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre.

question:
During a robotics practical in a technical training laboratory, a robotic arm initially rotates at 6 rad s⁻¹. To improve production efficiency, its controller increases the rotational speed uniformly to 18 rad s⁻¹ in 4 s.

What is the angular acceleration of the robotic arm?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to calculate the angular acceleration of a robotic arm whose rotational speed increases uniformly over a given time interval.
explanation.principle:
Before solving this question, it is important to understand what angular acceleration means.

Angular acceleration is the rate at which angular velocity changes with time. It tells us how quickly a rotating object speeds up or slows down.

In everyday life, many rotating machines do not instantly reach their operating speed. A ceiling fan, washing machine, electric motor, turbine and robotic arm all require some time to accelerate.

A robotic arm used in manufacturing performs repeated rotational movements while assembling, welding or sorting objects. When its controller increases the speed of rotation steadily, the arm experiences angular acceleration.

To calculate angular acceleration, we compare the change in angular velocity with the time taken for the change.
explanation.foundational_knowledge:
Rotational motion is the circular equivalent of linear motion.

In linear motion:
• Velocity describes how fast an object moves.
• Acceleration describes how quickly the velocity changes.

Similarly, in rotational motion:
• Angular velocity describes how fast an object rotates.
• Angular acceleration describes how quickly the angular velocity changes.

A positive angular acceleration means the object is rotating faster with time, while a negative angular acceleration (angular deceleration) means it is slowing down.
explanation.formula_introduction:
The fundamental formula for angular acceleration is:

α = (ω − ω₀) / t

where:
• α = angular acceleration (rad s⁻²)
• ω = final angular velocity (rad s⁻¹)
• ω₀ = initial angular velocity (rad s⁻¹)
• t = time taken (s)

This formula is derived from the definition of acceleration as the change in velocity divided by the time taken. It is used whenever the angular velocity changes uniformly.
explanation.related_formula_connections:
Before studying angular acceleration, students should understand angular velocity, given by:

ω = θ / t

where θ is the angular displacement.

After mastering angular acceleration, learners can progress to centripetal acceleration:

a = ω²r

which is then used to calculate centripetal force in circular motion.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known values.

Initial angular velocity, ω₀ = 6 rad s⁻¹
Final angular velocity, ω = 18 rad s⁻¹
Time, t = 4 s

Step 2: Identify the required quantity.

Required: Angular acceleration, α.

Step 3: Apply the appropriate formula.

α = (ω − ω₀) / t

Step 4: Substitute the values.

α = (18 − 6) / 4
α = 12 / 4

Step 5: Calculate the answer.

α = 3 rad s⁻²

Step 6: Compare with the options.

Option B gives 3 rad s⁻².
Therefore, Option B is the correct answer.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (2 rad s⁻²) results from an incorrect calculation of the change in angular velocity or an arithmetic error.
Option C (4 rad s⁻²) would be obtained if the time or the change in angular velocity were wrongly substituted into the formula.
Option D (6 rad s⁻²) incorrectly treats the angular acceleration as the numerical difference between the angular velocities without dividing by the time taken.
explanation.misconception_alert: A common mistake is to subtract the angular velocities correctly but forget to divide by the time. Another frequent error is to use the initial angular velocity alone instead of the change in angular velocity.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever you encounter an angular acceleration question, first identify the initial angular velocity, final angular velocity and time. Then use α = (ω − ω₀) / t. Always check that the unit of your answer is rad s⁻².
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine turning the speed control of a robotic arm. It does not suddenly jump to its new speed. Instead, its rotational speed increases gradually. Angular acceleration measures how quickly this increase happens. In this question, the speed increases by 12 rad s⁻¹ in 4 seconds, giving an angular acceleration of 3 rad s⁻².

question:
An automated warehouse in Lagos uses a rotating sorting platform to direct packages onto different conveyor belts. During a software-controlled speed adjustment, the platform's angular velocity increases uniformly from 9 rad s⁻¹ to 33 rad s⁻¹ in 8 s.

Which conclusion correctly describes the rotational motion of the platform during this interval?

options.A: The platform has an angular acceleration of 3.0 rad s⁻².
options.B: The platform has an angular acceleration of 4.1 rad s⁻².
options.C: The platform has an angular acceleration of 24 rad s⁻².
options.D: The platform has an angular acceleration of 264 rad s⁻².

answer_text: The platform has an angular acceleration of 3.0 rad s⁻².

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to determine the angular acceleration of a rotating automated sorting platform when its angular velocity changes uniformly over a given period of time.
explanation.principle:
Angular acceleration describes how quickly the angular velocity of a rotating object changes with time.

Before learning about forces in circular motion, it is important to understand how rotational speed changes.

A rotating object may speed up, slow down or rotate at a constant speed.

If its rotational speed increases steadily, it has a positive angular acceleration.

If its rotational speed decreases steadily, it has a negative angular acceleration, also called angular deceleration.

The SI unit of angular acceleration is rad s⁻².
explanation.foundational_knowledge:
Angular velocity tells us how fast an object rotates.

Angular acceleration tells us how quickly that rotational speed changes.

Examples include electric motors, robotic arms, conveyor systems, turbine shafts and automated warehouse sorters.

Whenever a rotating machine changes speed uniformly, its angular acceleration can be calculated from the change in angular velocity divided by the time taken.
explanation.formula_introduction:
The fundamental relationship is:

α = (ω − ω₀)/t

where:

α = angular acceleration (rad s⁻²)
ω = final angular velocity (rad s⁻¹)
ω₀ = initial angular velocity (rad s⁻¹)
t = time taken (s)

This formula is obtained because angular acceleration is defined as the rate of change of angular velocity with time.

A related equation is:

ω = ω₀ + αt

This equation is useful when any one of the four quantities is unknown.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

Initial angular velocity, ω₀ = 9 rad s⁻¹
Final angular velocity, ω = 33 rad s⁻¹
Time, t = 8 s

Step 2: Determine the required quantity.

We need the angular acceleration, α.

Step 3: Apply the formula.

α = (ω − ω₀)/t

Step 4: Substitute the values.

α = (33 − 9)/8

α = 24/8

α = 3.0 rad s⁻²

Step 5: Compare with the options.

Option A states 3.0 rad s⁻².

Therefore, Option A is correct.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because it does not result from the correct substitution into the angular acceleration formula.
Option C is incorrect because 24 rad s⁻¹ is the change in angular velocity, not the angular acceleration.
Option D is incorrect because it results from an incorrect mathematical operation involving the given quantities.
explanation.misconception_alert: A common mistake is to divide the final angular velocity by time instead of dividing the change in angular velocity by time. Always subtract the initial angular velocity from the final angular velocity before performing the division.
explanation.exam_tip: In JAMB examinations, carefully identify the initial angular velocity, final angular velocity and time before selecting a formula. Whenever the question asks how quickly rotational speed changes, the required quantity is angular acceleration.
explanation.final_answer: A. The platform has an angular acceleration of 3.0 rad s⁻².
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine a luggage carousel at an airport that starts rotating faster after receiving many bags. The increase in its rotational speed does not happen instantly. Angular acceleration measures how quickly that increase occurs. In this question, the platform's speed increases by 24 rad s⁻¹ over 8 seconds, giving an angular acceleration of 3.0 rad s⁻².

question:
A medical drone used to deliver emergency blood supplies starts its propeller from an angular velocity of 32 rad s⁻¹. To improve lift, the control system increases the propeller's angular velocity uniformly to 92 rad s⁻¹ in 6 s.

What is the angular acceleration of the propeller?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to calculate the angular acceleration of a rotating propeller when its angular velocity increases uniformly over a given time.
explanation.principle:
Angular acceleration describes how quickly the angular velocity of a rotating object changes with time.

A beginner may ask: What is angular velocity?

Angular velocity is the rate at which an object rotates. It tells us how fast the object turns and is measured in radians per second (rad s⁻¹).

Another beginner may ask: What is angular acceleration?

Angular acceleration is the rate of change of angular velocity. It indicates how rapidly a rotating object speeds up or slows down. It is measured in radians per second squared (rad s⁻²).

Whenever the angular velocity changes with time, angular acceleration is present.

Drone propellers, electric motors, turbine shafts and industrial machines all experience angular acceleration when starting up or changing speed.
explanation.formula_introduction:
The fundamental formula for angular acceleration is:

α = (ω − ω₀)/t

This formula means that angular acceleration equals the change in angular velocity divided by the time taken for that change.

Where:

• α = angular acceleration (rad s⁻²)
• ω = final angular velocity (rad s⁻¹)
• ω₀ = initial angular velocity (rad s⁻¹)
• t = time (s)

This is the rotational equivalent of linear acceleration, where acceleration equals change in velocity divided by time.
explanation.related_formula_connections:
Angular acceleration builds upon angular velocity.

Angular velocity itself can be obtained from:

ω = θ/t

where θ is the angular displacement.

After mastering angular acceleration, students will later study centripetal acceleration:

a = ω²r

which leads naturally to centripetal force in circular motion.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known values.

Initial angular velocity, ω₀ = 32 rad s⁻¹
Final angular velocity, ω = 92 rad s⁻¹
Time, t = 6 s

Step 2: State the required quantity.

Required: Angular acceleration, α.

Step 3: Write the appropriate formula.

α = (ω − ω₀)/t

Step 4: Substitute the values.

α = (92 − 32)/6

= 60/6

Step 5: Calculate the result.

α = 10 rad s⁻²

Step 6: Compare with the options.

Option B gives 10 rad s⁻², which agrees with the calculated value.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because it underestimates the rate of change of angular velocity. It could result from an arithmetic error when dividing.
Option C is incorrect because it overestimates the angular acceleration by using an incorrect change in angular velocity or time.
Option D is incorrect because it does not follow from the given values and may arise from using the wrong formula.
explanation.misconception_alert: Many students mistakenly divide the final angular velocity by the time without first finding the change in angular velocity. Always subtract the initial angular velocity from the final angular velocity before dividing by time.
explanation.exam_tip:
For JAMB questions on angular acceleration:

• Identify ω₀, ω and t first.
• Check whether the object is speeding up or slowing down.
• Use α = (ω − ω₀)/t.
• Always attach the correct unit, rad s⁻².
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine switching on a drone. Its propeller does not instantly reach full speed. Instead, it gradually spins faster. The rate at which its spinning speed increases is called the angular acceleration. In this case, the propeller's rotational speed increases by 60 rad s⁻¹ in 6 seconds, so its angular acceleration is 10 rad s⁻².
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Circular Motion → Angular Acceleration for a complete lesson on calculating angular acceleration, angular deceleration, rotational start-up, braking systems, electric motors, drone propellers and other JAMB-style worked examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for beginner-friendly explanations and additional practice.

question:
A food processing factory operates four production lines, each driven by a different rotating machine. During routine performance testing, the engineers recorded the angular acceleration of each machine as follows:

Machine I: Increased its angular velocity from 5 rad s⁻¹ to 29 rad s⁻¹ in 8 s.

Machine II: Increased its angular velocity from 7 rad s⁻¹ to 37 rad s⁻¹ in 12 s.

Machine III: Increased its angular velocity from 10 rad s⁻¹ to 34 rad s⁻¹ in 6 s.

Machine IV: Increased its angular velocity from 12 rad s⁻¹ to 33 rad s⁻¹ in 7 s.

Which machine has the greatest angular acceleration?

explanation.question_restate: This question compares the angular acceleration of four rotating machines in a food processing factory. You are required to determine which machine speeds up at the highest rate.
explanation.principle:
Angular acceleration describes how quickly the angular velocity of a rotating object changes with time.

Before studying angular acceleration, it is important to understand angular velocity.

Angular velocity tells us how fast an object rotates.

Angular acceleration tells us how rapidly that rotational speed changes.

For example, when an industrial mixer is switched on, it does not immediately reach its operating speed. Its rotational speed gradually increases. The rate of this increase is called angular acceleration.

Angular acceleration is a fundamental quantity in rotational motion and is widely used in analysing motors, turbines, conveyor systems and other rotating industrial equipment.
explanation.formula_introduction:
The fundamental formula for angular acceleration is:

α = (ω − ω₀)/t

where:

α = angular acceleration (rad s⁻²)
ω = final angular velocity (rad s⁻¹)
ω₀ = initial angular velocity (rad s⁻¹)
t = time taken (s)

This formula is obtained by dividing the change in angular velocity by the time taken for the change to occur.

A related formula is:

ω = ω₀ + αt

This equation is used when one of the quantities is unknown. In this question, however, the first formula is the most appropriate because the initial angular velocity, final angular velocity and time are all provided.
steps:
Step 1: Calculate the angular acceleration of Machine I.

α = (29 − 5)/8 = 24/8 = 3.0 rad s⁻².

Step 2: Calculate the angular acceleration of Machine II.

α = (37 − 7)/12 = 30/12 = 2.5 rad s⁻².

Step 3: Calculate the angular acceleration of Machine III.

α = (34 − 10)/6 = 24/6 = 4.0 rad s⁻².

Step 4: Calculate the angular acceleration of Machine IV.

α = (33 − 12)/7 = 21/7 = 3.0 rad s⁻².

Step 5: Compare the results.

Machine I = 3.0 rad s⁻²
Machine II = 2.5 rad s⁻²
Machine III = 4.0 rad s⁻²
Machine IV = 3.0 rad s⁻²

Machine III has the greatest angular acceleration.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because Machine I has an angular acceleration of 3.0 rad s⁻², which is less than that of Machine III.
Option B is incorrect because Machine II has the smallest angular acceleration, 2.5 rad s⁻².
Option D is incorrect because Machine IV has an angular acceleration of 3.0 rad s⁻², which is also less than that of Machine III.
explanation.misconception_alert: A common mistake is to compare only the increase in angular velocity without considering the time taken. Angular acceleration depends on both the change in angular velocity and the time over which the change occurs.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever several rotating systems are compared, calculate the angular acceleration for each one separately before deciding which is greatest. Do not assume that the largest increase in angular velocity always corresponds to the largest angular acceleration.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine four ceiling fans being switched on at the same time. The fan that reaches a much higher speed in the shortest time is accelerating rotationally the fastest. In the same way, Machine III increases its rotational speed most rapidly, so it has the greatest angular acceleration.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Circular Motion → Angular Acceleration to learn how to calculate angular acceleration, distinguish it from angular velocity, solve reverse calculations and analyse rotating machines commonly tested in JAMB-style examinations.

question:
An automotive manufacturing plant in Ogun State is evaluating four newly installed robotic wheel-assembly stations. During a quality assessment, each station accelerates a rotating platform from rest to its operating angular velocity.

The recorded data are:

Station I: Final angular velocity = 36 rad s⁻¹ in 6 s

Station II: Final angular velocity = 45 rad s⁻¹ in 9 s

Station III: Final angular velocity = 32 rad s⁻¹ in 4 s

Station IV: Final angular velocity = 54 rad s⁻¹ in 12 s

The engineering specification requires selecting the station with the greatest angular acceleration to achieve the fastest production start-up.

Which station best satisfies this requirement?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to evaluate the rotational performance of four automated manufacturing stations by determining which one has the greatest angular acceleration.
explanation.principle:
Angular acceleration describes how rapidly the angular velocity of a rotating object changes with time.

Imagine switching on different electric fans. Although they all begin from rest, some reach full speed much faster than others. The fan that gains rotational speed most quickly has the greater angular acceleration.

Angular acceleration is an important design parameter in robotics, automated manufacturing, electric motors, turbines and other rotating machines because faster acceleration often improves productivity.

Before solving any problem involving angular acceleration, identify:

• the initial angular velocity;
• the final angular velocity;
• the time taken for the change.

The object in this question starts from rest, so its initial angular velocity is zero.
explanation.foundational_knowledge:
Angular velocity measures how fast an object rotates and is measured in radians per second (rad s⁻¹).

Angular acceleration measures how quickly that angular velocity changes and is measured in radians per second squared (rad s⁻²).

A larger angular acceleration means the machine reaches its required operating speed more quickly.
explanation.formula_introduction:
The fundamental formula for angular acceleration is:

α = (ω − ω₀)/t

where:

• α = angular acceleration (rad s⁻²)
• ω = final angular velocity (rad s⁻¹)
• ω₀ = initial angular velocity (rad s⁻¹)
• t = time (s)

Since every station starts from rest, ω₀ = 0.

Therefore,

α = ω/t

This relationship is obtained by dividing the change in angular velocity by the time taken for that change.
explanation.related_formula_connections:
Students should already understand angular velocity:

ω = θ/t

where θ is angular displacement.

After mastering angular acceleration, the next major concept is centripetal acceleration:

a = ω²r

which eventually leads to centripetal force:

F = mv²/r.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the required quantity.

We are looking for the station with the greatest angular acceleration.

Step 2: Calculate each angular acceleration.

Station I:
α = 36/6 = 6 rad s⁻²

Station II:
α = 45/9 = 5 rad s⁻²

Station III:
α = 32/4 = 8 rad s⁻²

Station IV:
α = 54/12 = 4.5 rad s⁻²

Step 3: Compare the results.

Station III has the highest angular acceleration of 8 rad s⁻².

Step 4: Select the correct option.

Therefore, the correct answer is Station III.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because Station I has an angular acceleration of 6 rad s⁻², which is less than that of Station III.
Option B is incorrect because Station II has an angular acceleration of only 5 rad s⁻².
Option D is incorrect because although Station IV reaches a high final angular velocity, it takes much longer to do so, giving an angular acceleration of only 4.5 rad s⁻².
explanation.misconception_alert: Many students incorrectly choose the station with the highest final angular velocity. Angular acceleration depends on both the change in angular velocity and the time taken. A machine reaching a lower speed in a much shorter time may have a greater angular acceleration.
explanation.exam_tip: In JAMB examinations, comparison questions often require you to calculate values for all alternatives before making a decision. Never judge by the largest angular velocity alone. Always apply α = (ω − ω₀)/t.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine four cars starting from a traffic light. The car that gains speed the fastest is not necessarily the one that eventually reaches the highest speed. In the same way, Station III increases its rotational speed more quickly than the others, giving it the greatest angular acceleration.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Circular Motion → Angular Acceleration to learn how to calculate angular acceleration, distinguish it from angular velocity, analyse rotating machines, solve comparison problems and prepare for subsequent topics on centripetal acceleration and centripetal force.

question: During a Physics laboratory practical, a student whirls a small stone tied to one end of a string in a horizontal circle at a nearly constant speed. Which statement best explains the force that keeps the stone moving along the circular path?

options.A: An inward force directed towards the centre of the circle continuously changes the direction of the stone's motion.
options.B: An outward force continuously pushes the stone away from the centre of the circle.
options.C: The weight of the stone alone keeps it moving in the circular path.
options.D: No force is required because the stone already has a constant speed.

answer_text: An inward force directed towards the centre of the circle continuously changes the direction of the stone's motion.

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your understanding of the meaning of centripetal force and why it is required whenever an object moves in a circular path.
explanation.principle:
Before solving this question, it is important to understand what centripetal force means.

When an object moves in a circle, its speed may remain constant, but its direction changes continuously. Since velocity depends on both speed and direction, a continual change in direction means the object is accelerating.

According to Newton's Second Law, every acceleration must be produced by a force. The force responsible for continuously changing the direction of motion towards the centre of the circle is called the centripetal force.

The word centripetal means centre-seeking.

Without this inward force, the object would leave the circular path and continue in a straight line tangent to the circle because of inertia.
explanation.foundational_knowledge:
A force is a push or pull that can change the motion of an object.

Velocity is a vector quantity because it has both magnitude and direction.

Even if the magnitude of the velocity remains constant, a change in direction means the velocity has changed. Therefore, circular motion always involves acceleration.
explanation.formula_introduction:
Although this question is conceptual, the fundamental equation for centripetal force is:

F = mv²/r

where:

F = centripetal force in newtons (N)

m = mass of the object in kilograms (kg)

v = linear speed in metres per second (m s⁻¹)

r = radius of the circular path in metres (m)

This formula is derived by combining Newton's Second Law, F = ma, with the centripetal acceleration, a = v²/r.
explanation.related_formula_connections:
Students should also know these related relationships:

• Centripetal acceleration: a = v²/r

• Newton's Second Law: F = ma

Combining these gives:

F = m(v²/r) = mv²/r.

Later in this topic, you will also learn that if angular velocity is known, linear speed is given by v = ωr, leading to another useful expression: F = mω²r.
steps:
Step 1: Identify what keeps an object moving in a circle.

An object moving in a circular path must have a force acting towards the centre of the circle.

Step 2: Examine Option A.

It correctly states that an inward force directed towards the centre continuously changes the direction of motion. This is the definition of centripetal force.

Step 3: Examine Option B.

An outward force is not what keeps an object moving in a circle. The apparent outward effect experienced in a rotating frame is associated with centrifugal effects, not the real force maintaining circular motion.

Step 4: Examine Option C.

The weight of the stone acts vertically downward. It is not generally the force that keeps the stone moving around the circle in this situation. Here, the tension in the string provides the required inward force.

Step 5: Examine Option D.

Even though the speed is constant, the direction changes continuously. Therefore, an inward force is still required.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because circular motion requires an inward force, not an outward one.
Option C is incorrect because the stone's weight does not provide the required inward force in this laboratory setup. The tension in the string does.
Option D is incorrect because constant speed does not mean zero force during circular motion. The changing direction means the object is accelerating.
explanation.misconception_alert: Many students think that because an object moves with constant speed, no force acts on it. This is incorrect. In circular motion, the direction changes continuously, so an inward force must always act on the object.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever you encounter a question on circular motion, remember that the centripetal force always acts towards the centre of the circular path. If this force disappears, the object moves off along a straight-line path tangent to the circle.
explanation.final_answer: A. An inward force directed towards the centre of the circle continuously changes the direction of the stone's motion.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine swinging a stone tied to a string around your hand. The string keeps pulling the stone towards your hand. That inward pull keeps changing the stone's direction so it follows a circle. If the string breaks, the stone flies off in a straight line instead of continuing around the circle.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Circular Motion → Centripetal Force to learn the meaning, direction and applications of centripetal force, together with worked JAMB-style examples, beginner-friendly explanations and practice questions available in the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre.

question: During a physics laboratory demonstration, a rubber stopper is whirled at a constant speed in a horizontal circle using a string. At every point along its circular path, in which direction does the centripetal force acting on the rubber stopper always act?

options.A: Tangential to the circular path in the direction of motion
options.B: Radially outward from the centre of the circle
options.C: Towards the centre of the circle

answer_text: Towards the centre of the circle

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your understanding of the direction in which centripetal force acts on an object moving in a circular path.
explanation.principle:
Before answering this question, it is important to understand what centripetal force is.

Whenever an object moves in a circle, its direction changes continuously, even if its speed remains constant. Since velocity depends on both speed and direction, a continuous change in direction means the object is accelerating.

This inward acceleration is called centripetal acceleration. According to Newton's Second Law, every acceleration must be produced by a force. The force responsible for producing this inward acceleration is called the centripetal force.

The word centripetal means centre-seeking. Therefore, the centripetal force always acts towards the centre of the circular path.

If this inward force were removed, the object would no longer move in a circle. Instead, it would continue in a straight line tangent to the circle because of inertia.
explanation.foundational_knowledge:
An object moving in a circle is constantly changing direction.

A change in direction means there is a change in velocity.

A change in velocity means there is acceleration.

This acceleration always points towards the centre of the circle and is called centripetal acceleration.

The force that produces this acceleration must also point towards the centre.
explanation.formula_introduction:
Although no calculation is required here, the fundamental equation for centripetal force is:

F = mv²/r

where:

F = centripetal force in newtons (N)

m = mass of the object in kilograms (kg)

v = linear speed in metres per second (m s⁻¹)

r = radius of the circular path in metres (m)

This formula is used to calculate the magnitude of the centripetal force. Regardless of its magnitude, its direction is always towards the centre of the circular path.
explanation.related_formula_connections:
The centripetal acceleration is given by:

a = v²/r

Since F = ma, substituting the centripetal acceleration into Newton's Second Law gives:

F = m(v²/r)

which simplifies to:

F = mv²/r.

These relationships show that centripetal force and centripetal acceleration always have the same inward direction.
steps:
Step 1: Identify what the question asks.

The question is asking for the direction of the centripetal force, not its magnitude.

Step 2: Recall the definition.

Centripetal force is the inward force that keeps an object moving along a circular path.

Step 3: Determine the correct option.

Since the force always acts towards the centre of the circle, the correct answer is Option C.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because the tangential direction is the direction the object would move if the centripetal force suddenly disappeared. It is not the direction of the centripetal force.
Option B is incorrect because an outward direction describes the apparent centrifugal effect observed in a rotating frame, not the actual centripetal force acting on the object.
Option D is incorrect because centripetal force is not generally directed vertically upward. Its direction depends on the position of the centre of the circular path.
explanation.misconception_alert: Many students confuse centripetal force with centrifugal force. In an inertial frame, the real force responsible for circular motion is the centripetal force, and it always acts towards the centre of the circle.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever a JAMB question asks for the direction of centripetal force, immediately think inward towards the centre. If you see an option stating tangential or outward, examine it carefully because these are common distractors.
explanation.final_answer: C. Towards the centre of the circle
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine swinging a stone tied to a string. The string keeps pulling the stone towards your hand while the stone keeps moving around the circle. That inward pull provided by the string is the centripetal force. Without it, the stone would fly off in a straight line.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Circular Motion → Centripetal Force to learn why objects require an inward force to move in circles, how centripetal force differs from centrifugal effects, and how to solve JAMB-style conceptual and calculation questions.

question: A bucket containing water is swung in a vertical circle of radius 1.2 m at a constant speed of 6.0 m s⁻¹. If the combined mass of the bucket and water is 2.4 kg, what centripetal force is required to keep the bucket moving along the circular path?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to calculate the centripetal force needed to keep a bucket of water moving in a vertical circular path.
explanation.principle:
Whenever an object moves in a circle, its direction changes continuously even if its speed remains constant. Since velocity depends on both speed and direction, the changing direction means the object is accelerating. This inward acceleration is called centripetal acceleration. According to Newton's Second Law of Motion, every acceleration must be produced by a force. The inward force responsible for keeping an object on its circular path is called centripetal force.

A beginner may ask why the bucket does not simply fly away. The answer is that an inward force continuously pulls the bucket toward the centre of the circle. If this inward force disappears, the bucket moves off in a straight line tangent to the circle because of inertia.
explanation.foundational_knowledge:
Before solving centripetal force problems, identify the following quantities:

• Mass of the object (m) in kilograms (kg).
• Speed of the object (v) in metres per second (m s⁻¹).
• Radius of the circular path (r) in metres (m).

These quantities are substituted into the centripetal force formula.
explanation.formula_introduction:
The fundamental formula for centripetal force is:

F = mv²/r

where:

• F = centripetal force in newtons (N).
• m = mass in kilograms (kg).
• v = speed in metres per second (m s⁻¹).
• r = radius of the circular path in metres (m).

The formula shows that centripetal force increases with mass and with the square of the speed, but decreases as the radius increases.
explanation.related_formula_connections:
Closely related formulas include:

• Centripetal acceleration: a = v²/r.
• Newton's Second Law: F = ma.

Combining these gives F = m(v²/r), which simplifies to F = mv²/r. If angular velocity is known instead of linear speed, the relationship v = ωr may first be used.
steps:
Step 1: Write down the known values.

Mass, m = 2.4 kg
Speed, v = 6.0 m s⁻¹
Radius, r = 1.2 m

Step 2: Identify the required quantity.

Required: Centripetal force, F.

Step 3: Apply the formula.

F = mv²/r

Step 4: Substitute the given values.

F = (2.4 × 6.0²)/1.2

= (2.4 × 36)/1.2

= 86.4/1.2

= 72.0 N

Step 5: Compare the calculated value with the options.

72.0 N corresponds to Option C.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (43.2 N) is incorrect because it results from an incorrect substitution or arithmetic error.
Option B (57.6 N) is incorrect because the calculation does not correctly apply the formula F = mv²/r.
Option D (86.4 N) is incorrect because it is the numerator before dividing by the radius of 1.2 m.
explanation.misconception_alert: A common mistake is to forget that the speed is squared. Another common error is to stop after multiplying m by v² without dividing by the radius.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever you are given the mass, speed and radius of an object moving in a circle, immediately think of the formula F = mv²/r. Pay close attention to the square of the speed because it has the greatest effect on the final answer.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine swinging a bucket of water over your head. Your hand must keep pulling the bucket toward the centre of its path. The stronger this inward pull, the easier it is for the bucket to remain on its circular path. In this case, the required inward pull is 72.0 N.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Circular Motion → Centripetal Force to learn how to calculate centripetal force, centripetal acceleration, speed and radius, with beginner-friendly worked examples and additional JAMB-style practice questions.

question: A motorcycle of mass 180 kg negotiates a bend in a residential estate at a constant speed of 12 m s⁻¹. If the radius of the bend is 36 m, what is the centripetal force acting on the motorcycle?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to calculate the centripetal force needed to keep a motorcycle moving round a curved road at a constant speed.
explanation.principle:
Before solving this question, it is important to understand what centripetal force means.

Whenever an object moves in a circular path, its direction changes continuously, even if its speed remains constant. Since velocity depends on both speed and direction, this continuous change in direction means the object is accelerating.

The inward force responsible for producing this acceleration is called the centripetal force. Without this inward force, the motorcycle would continue moving in a straight line due to inertia instead of following the bend.

For a motorcycle negotiating a bend, the friction between the tyres and the road provides the required centripetal force.
explanation.formula_introduction:
The fundamental formula for centripetal force is:

F = mv²/r

where:

• F = centripetal force in newtons (N)
• m = mass in kilograms (kg)
• v = linear speed in metres per second (m s⁻¹)
• r = radius of the circular path in metres (m)

This equation is obtained by combining Newton's Second Law, F = ma, with the expression for centripetal acceleration, a = v²/r.
explanation.related_formula_connections:
Some related formulas are:

• Centripetal acceleration: a = v²/r
• Angular velocity: ω = v/r
• Alternative centripetal force formula: F = mω²r

When linear speed is given, F = mv²/r is the most direct formula to use.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known values.

Mass, m = 180 kg
Speed, v = 12 m s⁻¹
Radius, r = 36 m

Step 2: Identify the required quantity.

Required: Centripetal force, F.

Step 3: Write the appropriate formula.

F = mv²/r

Step 4: Substitute the values.

F = (180 × 12²)/36

= (180 × 144)/36

= 25920/36

Step 5: Calculate the answer.

F = 720 N

Step 6: Verify with the options.

720 N corresponds to Option C.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (360 N) is incorrect because it is only half of the correct value and may result from an arithmetic error during division.
Option B (540 N) is incorrect because it results from an incorrect substitution or multiplication.
Option D (900 N) is incorrect because it overestimates the required centripetal force due to a calculation mistake.
explanation.misconception_alert: A common mistake is to forget to square the speed. Since the formula contains v², even a small increase in speed greatly increases the required centripetal force.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine riding a motorcycle round a bend. Something must continuously pull the motorcycle towards the centre of the curve; otherwise, it would move straight ahead. That inward pull is the centripetal force. In this case, the required inward force is 720 N.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Circular Motion → Centripetal Force to learn how to calculate centripetal force, centripetal acceleration, speed, radius and angular velocity using fully worked JAMB-style examples and beginner-friendly explanations at the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre.

question: A cyclist rides round a circular playground at a constant speed of 8 m s⁻¹. If the bicycle-and-rider have a combined mass of 60 kg and the required centripetal force is 240 N, what is the radius of the circular path?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to determine the radius of a circular path when the mass of the object, its speed and the centripetal force acting on it are known.
explanation.principle:
Whenever an object moves in a circle, its direction changes continuously even if its speed remains constant. Since velocity depends on both speed and direction, this continual change in direction means the object is accelerating. This inward acceleration is called centripetal acceleration. According to Newton's Second Law of Motion, every acceleration is produced by a force. The inward force that keeps an object moving in a circular path is called the centripetal force.

Before solving any centripetal-force problem, first identify the known quantities, determine the required quantity and then choose the appropriate formula.
explanation.foundational_knowledge:
The fundamental equation for centripetal force is:

F = mv²/r

where:
F = centripetal force in newtons (N)
m = mass in kilograms (kg)
v = linear speed in metres per second (m s⁻¹)
r = radius of the circular path in metres (m)

This formula shows that centripetal force increases with mass and with the square of speed, but decreases as the radius increases.

When the radius is unknown, rearrange the equation to obtain:

r = mv²/F
explanation.related_formula_connections:
Students should also know that:

• Centripetal acceleration: a = v²/r

• Since F = ma, substituting a = v²/r gives F = mv²/r.

• If angular velocity is given instead of linear speed, use v = ωr before applying the centripetal-force equation.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known values.

Mass, m = 60 kg
Speed, v = 8 m s⁻¹
Centripetal force, F = 240 N

Step 2: Identify the required quantity.

Radius, r

Step 3: Select the appropriate formula.

r = mv²/F

Step 4: Substitute the values.

r = (60 × 8²)/240

= (60 × 64)/240

= 3840/240

Step 5: Calculate.

r = 16 m

Step 6: Compare with the options.

16.0 m corresponds to Option B.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (12.0 m) results from an incorrect substitution or arithmetic error.
Option C (20.0 m) is obtained if the formula is incorrectly manipulated or the square of the speed is not handled properly.
Option D (24.0 m) overestimates the radius and does not satisfy the centripetal-force equation using the given values.
explanation.misconception_alert: A common mistake is to use r = F/(mv²) instead of rearranging the formula correctly. Another frequent error is forgetting to square the speed before substitution.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever JAMB asks you to determine the radius in a centripetal-force problem, start with F = mv²/r and rearrange it before substituting the numerical values. Always square the speed first before multiplying by the mass.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine riding a bicycle around a circular playground. If you move faster, a stronger inward force is needed to keep you on the circular path. For a given speed and force, the size of the circle can be calculated using the centripetal-force formula. In this case, the bicycle moves round a circle of radius 16 m.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Circular Motion → Centripetal Force → Determining Radius for beginner-friendly lessons, formula derivations, worked examples, reverse calculations and additional JAMB-style practice questions available in the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre.

question: A car of mass 900 kg negotiates a roundabout in Port Harcourt along a circular path of radius 45 m. If the centripetal force acting on the car is 3,600 N, what is the speed of the car?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to determine the speed of a car moving in a circular path when its mass, the radius of the path and the centripetal force are known.
explanation.principle:
Whenever an object moves in a circular path, its direction changes continuously, even if its speed remains constant. Since velocity depends on both speed and direction, this continuous change in direction means the object is accelerating. This inward acceleration is called centripetal acceleration.

According to Newton's Second Law of Motion, every acceleration is produced by a force. The force that keeps an object moving round a circle is called the centripetal force. Without this inward force, the object would move off in a straight line tangent to the circular path.

The fundamental equation for centripetal force is:

F = mv²/r

where:
F = centripetal force in newtons (N)
m = mass in kilograms (kg)
v = speed in metres per second (m s⁻¹)
r = radius of the circular path in metres (m)

This is the fundamental formula used whenever the mass, speed, radius or centripetal force is to be determined.

Related formulas include:

• Centripetal acceleration: a = v²/r
• Newton's Second Law: F = ma
• Angular velocity relationship: v = ωr

In this question, the speed is unknown, so the centripetal force formula is rearranged to make v the subject:

v = √(Fr/m).
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

Mass, m = 900 kg
Radius, r = 45 m
Centripetal force, F = 3,600 N
Required quantity = Speed, v

Step 2: Write the appropriate formula.

F = mv²/r

Rearranging for speed:

v = √(Fr/m)

Step 3: Substitute the given values.

v = √((3,600 × 45)/900)

Step 5: Compare the result with the options.

13.42 m s⁻¹ is approximately 13.4 m s⁻¹.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option B.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (12 m s⁻¹) is incorrect because it underestimates the speed required to produce the given centripetal force.
Option C (15 m s⁻¹) is incorrect because substituting this value into F = mv²/r gives a force greater than 3,600 N.
Option D (18 m s⁻¹) is incorrect because it would require a much larger centripetal force than the value given in the question.
explanation.misconception_alert: A common mistake is to substitute the values correctly but forget to take the square root after rearranging the formula. Another common error is to use F = ma directly without recognising that the acceleration involved is the centripetal acceleration, a = v²/r.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever JAMB asks you to determine the speed in a centripetal force problem, first identify the known quantities, rearrange the formula carefully, and remember that speed is squared. Always take the square root at the final stage of the calculation.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine driving round a roundabout. The faster you drive, the greater the inward force needed to keep the car on the curved path. Knowing the force, the mass of the car and the size of the roundabout allows you to calculate the speed at which the car is travelling.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Circular Motion → Centripetal Force to learn how to calculate centripetal force, speed, radius and mass using worked JAMB-style examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for beginner-friendly lessons, concept summaries and additional exam-focused practice.

question: A conveyor belt in a beverage factory carries cartons around a circular section of radius 2.5 m at a constant speed of 5.0 m s⁻¹. If the inward centripetal force acting on one carton is 30 N, what is the mass of the carton?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to determine the mass of a carton moving in a circular path when the centripetal force, speed and radius of motion are known.
explanation.principle:
Before solving this problem, it is important to understand what centripetal force is.

Whenever an object moves in a circular path, its direction changes continuously, even if its speed remains constant. Since velocity depends on both speed and direction, a continuous change in direction means the object is accelerating.

This inward acceleration is called centripetal acceleration, and the force responsible for producing it is called centripetal force.

In everyday life, conveyor systems in factories often guide products around curved sections. The side guides or mechanical supports provide the inward force that keeps the cartons following the circular path instead of moving off in a straight line.
explanation.foundational_knowledge:
Newton's Second Law states that every acceleration is produced by a force. In circular motion, the required inward acceleration is called centripetal acceleration and is given by:

a = v²/r

Since F = ma, substituting the expression for centripetal acceleration gives:

F = mv²/r

This is the fundamental formula used to solve most centripetal force problems.
explanation.formula_introduction:
The centripetal force formula is:

F = mv²/r

where:

F = centripetal force (newton, N)
m = mass (kilogram, kg)
v = linear speed (metre per second, m s⁻¹)
r = radius of the circular path (metre, m)

In this question, the unknown quantity is the mass, so rearrange the formula:

m = Fr/v²
explanation.related_formula_connections:
Students should also remember:

• Centripetal acceleration: a = v²/r
• Newton's Second Law: F = ma
• Combining the two equations gives F = mv²/r.

If angular velocity is provided instead of linear speed, then v = ωr may first be used before calculating the centripetal force.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known values.

F = 30 N
v = 5.0 m s⁻¹
r = 2.5 m

Step 2: State the required quantity.

Mass, m.

Step 3: Use the rearranged formula.

m = Fr/v²

Step 4: Substitute the given values.

m = (30 × 2.5)/(5.0²)
= 75/25
= 3.0 kg

Step 5: Compare with the options.

The calculated mass is 3.0 kg.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option C.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (2.0 kg) is incorrect because it underestimates the required mass for the given force and speed.
Option B (2.5 kg) is incorrect because the calculation using the centripetal force formula does not produce this value.
Option D (3.5 kg) is incorrect because it overestimates the mass needed to produce the given centripetal force.
explanation.misconception_alert: A common mistake is to substitute the values correctly but forget to square the speed. Another frequent error is rearranging the formula incorrectly by placing the radius in the denominator instead of the numerator.
explanation.exam_tip: When asked to determine mass in circular motion, first rearrange the formula before substituting the values. Always square the speed before carrying out the division, and remember to include the correct SI unit of kilogram.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine pushing a shopping trolley around a curved path. A heavier trolley needs a greater inward push to stay on the bend at the same speed. In this question, the inward force, speed and radius are known, so they can be used to calculate the carton's mass, which is 3.0 kg.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Circular Motion → Centripetal Force for a complete lesson on calculating centripetal force, centripetal acceleration, mass, speed and radius using worked JAMB-style examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for beginner-friendly lessons, concept summaries and additional exam-focused practice.

question:
A commercial bus of mass 1,200 kg is negotiating a highway curve of constant radius at a speed of 12 m s⁻¹. The driver later increases the speed to 24 m s⁻¹ while taking the same curve.

Assuming the radius of the curve remains unchanged, the centripetal force required to keep the bus on the circular path becomes:

explanation.question_restate: This question examines how changing the speed of an object moving in a circular path affects the centripetal force required to keep it on that path when the mass and radius remain unchanged.
explanation.principle:
Before solving this question, it is important to understand why centripetal force is needed.

Whenever an object moves in a circle, its direction changes continuously even if its speed is constant. Since velocity depends on both speed and direction, this continuous change in direction means the object is accelerating. The inward acceleration is called centripetal acceleration.

According to Newton's Second Law, every acceleration must be produced by a force. The inward force responsible for keeping the object moving in a circle is called the centripetal force.

The fundamental formula is:

F = mv²/r

where:

• F = centripetal force in newtons (N)
• m = mass in kilograms (kg)
• v = linear speed in metres per second (m s⁻¹)
• r = radius of the circular path in metres (m)

This is a fundamental relationship for uniform circular motion. It shows that centripetal force is directly proportional to the mass, directly proportional to the square of the speed, and inversely proportional to the radius.

A related formula is the centripetal acceleration:

a = v²/r

Since F = ma, substituting the centripetal acceleration gives F = mv²/r.

One important consequence is that if only the speed changes while the mass and radius remain constant, the centripetal force changes according to the square of the speed.
steps:
Step 1: Identify what changes.

The speed increases from 12 m s⁻¹ to 24 m s⁻¹.

The mass and radius remain constant.

Step 2: Use the proportional relationship.

Since F ∝ v²,

New force / Original force = (24/12)².

Step 3: Simplify the ratio.

24/12 = 2.

Therefore,

(2)² = 4.

Step 4: Draw the conclusion.

The required centripetal force becomes four times the original value.

Therefore, Option C is correct.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because doubling the speed does not merely double the centripetal force. The force depends on the square of the speed.
Option B is incorrect because there is no square-law relationship that produces a factor of three in this situation.
Option D is incorrect because increasing the speed by a factor of two increases the force by four, not eight.
explanation.misconception_alert: A common mistake is to assume that if the speed doubles, the centripetal force also doubles. Because the speed is squared in the formula, doubling the speed actually makes the required centripetal force four times greater.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever a JAMB question asks about the effect of changing speed while the mass and radius remain constant, avoid substituting numbers immediately. Use the proportional relationship F ∝ v². This saves time and reduces calculation errors.
explanation.final_answer: C. Four times the original value
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine swinging a bucket of water tied to a rope. If you swing it much faster while keeping the length of the rope the same, you must pull much harder on the rope to keep the bucket moving in a circle. That extra pull is the increased centripetal force. Doubling the speed requires four times as much inward force.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Circular Motion → Centripetal Force to learn why centripetal force depends on the square of speed, how to solve proportional reasoning questions, and how these ideas apply to vehicles negotiating bends, amusement rides and rotating machinery. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for beginner-friendly lessons and additional JAMB-style practice.

question:
Engineers evaluating a circular test track for vehicle inspection observe that a test car travels at a constant speed of 18 m s⁻¹ while negotiating different circular lanes.

If the radius of the lane is increased from 36 m to 72 m without changing the mass or speed of the car, what happens to the centripetal force required to keep the car moving in the circular path?

options.C: It becomes half of its original value.
options.D: It becomes four times its original value.

answer_text: It becomes half of its original value.

explanation.question_restate: This question examines how changing the radius of a circular path affects the centripetal force when the mass and speed remain constant.
explanation.principle:
Before answering this question, it is important to understand what centripetal force means.

Whenever an object moves in a circle, its direction changes continuously even if its speed remains constant. Since velocity depends on both speed and direction, a change in direction means the object is accelerating.

This inward acceleration is called centripetal acceleration, and according to Newton's Second Law, every acceleration must be produced by a force. The inward force responsible for keeping an object moving in a circular path is called the centripetal force.

The fundamental formula is:

F = mv²/r

where:

• F = centripetal force in newtons (N)
• m = mass in kilograms (kg)
• v = speed in metres per second (m s⁻¹)
• r = radius of the circular path in metres (m)

This is a fundamental relationship for uniform circular motion. It shows that centripetal force is directly proportional to mass and the square of speed, but inversely proportional to the radius.

Related formulas include:

• Centripetal acceleration: a = v²/r
• Relationship between linear and angular speed: v = ωr

Understanding these relationships helps explain why wider curves require less centripetal force at the same speed.
steps:
Step 1: Identify what changes.

The mass and speed remain constant.

Only the radius changes from 36 m to 72 m.

Step 2: Recall the formula.

F = mv²/r

Since m and v are unchanged, the force depends only on the radius.

Step 3: Compare the radii.

The radius is doubled:

72/36 = 2

Step 4: Apply proportional reasoning.

Because force is inversely proportional to radius,

doubling the radius reduces the required centripetal force to one-half of its original value.

Step 5: Verify with the options.

The correct statement is that the centripetal force becomes half of its original value.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because doubling the radius does not double the centripetal force. It reduces it.
Option B is incorrect because changing the radius changes the centripetal force when the speed and mass remain constant.

Option D is incorrect because increasing the radius does not make the force four times larger. A fourfold increase in force is more closely associated with doubling the speed, since force depends on the square of speed.
explanation.misconception_alert: Many students mistakenly believe that a larger circular path requires a larger centripetal force. In reality, when speed and mass are unchanged, a larger radius means the object turns more gently, so less inward force is needed.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever only the radius changes, avoid unnecessary calculations. Remember that centripetal force is inversely proportional to the radius. If the radius doubles, the force halves. If the radius halves, the force doubles.
explanation.final_answer: C. It becomes half of its original value.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine riding a bicycle around two circular tracks at the same speed. One track is small, while the other is much larger. On the larger track, you make a gentler turn, so less inward force is needed to keep you moving in the circle. That is why increasing the radius reduces the required centripetal force.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Circular Motion → Centripetal Force → Effect of Radius on Centripetal Force for a beginner-friendly explanation, proportional reasoning techniques, worked examples, and additional JAMB-style practice questions available in the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre.

subtopic: Effect of changing mass on centripetal force
question: An airport luggage carousel rotates at a constant speed of 2.5 m s⁻¹ along a circular track of radius 4.0 m. A suitcase of mass 12 kg is replaced with another suitcase of mass 18 kg while the speed and radius remain unchanged. What is the centripetal force required to keep the heavier suitcase moving in the circular path?

explanation.question_restate: This question examines how changing the mass of an object affects the centripetal force required to keep it moving in a circular path when the speed and radius remain constant.
explanation.principle:
Before solving this problem, it is important to understand what centripetal force means.

Whenever an object moves in a circle, its direction changes continuously even if its speed remains constant. Since velocity depends on both speed and direction, the object is continuously accelerating. This inward acceleration is called centripetal acceleration.

According to Newton's Second Law, every acceleration must be produced by a force. The inward force responsible for keeping an object on its circular path is called the centripetal force.

A beginner may ask why a heavier object needs more centripetal force.

The reason is that a greater mass possesses greater inertia. Therefore, a larger inward force is required to continually change its direction of motion while it travels round the circle.
explanation.foundational_knowledge:
The fundamental formula for centripetal force is:

F = mv²/r

where:

F = centripetal force (newton, N)
m = mass (kilogram, kg)
v = linear speed (metre per second, m s⁻¹)
r = radius of the circular path (metre, m)

This is a fundamental relationship obtained by combining Newton's Second Law, F = ma, with the expression for centripetal acceleration, a = v²/r.

The formula shows that centripetal force:

• increases directly with mass,
• increases with the square of speed,
• decreases as the radius increases.

Related formulas include:

• Centripetal acceleration: a = v²/r
• Linear speed and angular velocity: v = ωr

Students should first understand these relationships before solving circular motion problems.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known values.

Mass, m = 18 kg
Speed, v = 2.5 m s⁻¹
Radius, r = 4.0 m

Step 2: Write the appropriate formula.

F = mv²/r

Step 3: Square the speed.

v² = (2.5)² = 6.25

Step 4: Substitute the values.

F = (18 × 6.25)/4

F = 112.5/4

F = 28.125 N

Step 5: Round the answer appropriately.

F ≈ 28.13 N

This corresponds to Option B.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (11.25 N) results from an incorrect substitution and underestimates the required force.
Option C (16.88 N) is obtained from an incorrect manipulation of the formula or arithmetic error.
Option D (50.63 N) overestimates the force and does not agree with the correct calculation using F = mv²/r.
explanation.misconception_alert: A common mistake is to think that changing the mass does not affect centripetal force. From the formula F = mv²/r, the force is directly proportional to the mass when speed and radius remain constant. Another common error is forgetting to square the speed before substituting into the formula.
explanation.exam_tip: When a question states that only the mass changes while the speed and radius remain constant, remember that the centripetal force changes in the same ratio as the mass. Always identify which quantity changes before carrying out any calculation.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine two identical bags on the same airport luggage carousel. One is much heavier than the other. Since both travel along the same circular path at the same speed, the heavier bag needs a stronger inward pull to keep changing direction. That extra inward pull is the centripetal force, which is why the heavier suitcase requires 28.13 N.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Circular Motion → Centripetal Force to learn how mass, speed and radius affect centripetal force, with beginner-friendly explanations, worked examples and additional JAMB-style practice questions available in the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre.

question:
At a public park, a children's merry-go-round rotates uniformly. A child standing on the platform moves in a circular path of radius 2.5 m at a constant speed of 4.0 m s⁻¹.

What is the centripetal acceleration of the child?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to calculate the centripetal acceleration of an object moving at constant speed in a circular path using the radius and linear speed provided.
explanation.principle:
Before solving this question, it is important to understand what centripetal acceleration means.

When an object moves in a circle, its speed may remain constant, but its direction changes continuously. Since velocity depends on both speed and direction, the velocity is constantly changing.

A change in velocity means the object is accelerating.

This inward acceleration, directed towards the centre of the circle, is called centripetal acceleration.

It is responsible for keeping the object on its circular path.

If this inward acceleration did not exist, the object would move away along a straight-line path tangent to the circle due to inertia.
explanation.foundational_knowledge:
Before studying centripetal acceleration, you should understand that:

• Speed tells how fast an object moves.
• Velocity tells both how fast and in which direction it moves.
• An object moving in a circle is constantly changing direction.
• Therefore, even at constant speed, circular motion involves acceleration.
explanation.formula_introduction:
The fundamental formula for centripetal acceleration is:

a = v²/r

where:

• a = centripetal acceleration (m s⁻²)
• v = linear speed (m s⁻¹)
• r = radius of the circular path (m)

This formula is derived from the geometry of uniform circular motion and is used whenever an object moves round a circle at constant speed.

A related formula is:

F = ma

Combining this with centripetal acceleration gives:

F = mv²/r

This relationship will be applied in later questions involving centripetal force.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known values.

Radius, r = 2.5 m

Speed, v = 4.0 m s⁻¹

Step 2: Identify the required quantity.

Required: Centripetal acceleration, a.

Step 3: Write the appropriate formula.

a = v²/r

Step 4: Substitute the given values.

a = (4.0)²/2.5

= 16/2.5

= 6.4 m s⁻²

Step 5: Verify with the options.

Option C corresponds to 6.4 m s⁻².

Therefore, Option C is correct.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because it results from an incorrect substitution or arithmetic error.
Option B is incorrect because it does not equal v²/r for the given values.
Option D is incorrect because it overestimates the acceleration and is not obtained using the correct formula.
explanation.misconception_alert: Many students mistakenly think that constant speed means zero acceleration. This is true only for straight-line motion. In circular motion, the continuous change in direction means that acceleration is always present, even when the speed remains constant.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever a question asks for centripetal acceleration and gives the speed and radius, immediately recall the formula a = v²/r. Always square the speed before dividing by the radius, and ensure that SI units are used throughout.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine riding a merry-go-round. Even if you move at the same speed all the time, you constantly feel yourself being pulled towards the centre of the ride. This inward pull is associated with centripetal acceleration, which keeps you moving in a circle instead of flying off in a straight line.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Circular Motion → Centripetal Acceleration for a beginner-friendly lesson on centripetal acceleration, its relationship with velocity and radius, derivation of a = v²/r, worked examples, common mistakes, and JAMB-style practice questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for comprehensive concept reviews and exam-focused exercises.

subtopic: Using F = ma with circular motion
question: A rotating platform in a technical college workshop is used to demonstrate uniform circular motion. A metal block of mass 3.0 kg is placed on the platform and moves in a circle of radius 1.2 m. If its centripetal acceleration is 15 m s⁻², what is the centripetal force acting on the block?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your understanding of how Newton's Second Law applies to circular motion. Instead of calculating the acceleration from speed and radius, the centripetal acceleration is already provided, so you only need to determine the corresponding centripetal force.
explanation.principle:
Before solving this question, it is important to understand why an object moving in a circle experiences a force.

Whenever an object moves in a circular path, its direction changes continuously, even if its speed remains constant. Since velocity depends on both speed and direction, a continuous change in direction means the object is accelerating.

This inward acceleration is called centripetal acceleration.

According to Newton's Second Law, every acceleration is produced by a force. Therefore, the inward force responsible for producing centripetal acceleration is called the centripetal force.
explanation.foundational_knowledge:
Newton's Second Law states that the force acting on an object equals the product of its mass and acceleration.

In straight-line motion, this is written as:

F = ma

The same law applies to circular motion. The only difference is that the acceleration is now the centripetal acceleration directed towards the centre of the circular path.
explanation.formula_introduction:
The fundamental formula used is:

F = ma

where:

• F = force in newtons (N)
• m = mass in kilograms (kg)
• a = acceleration in metres per second squared (m s⁻²)

For circular motion, the acceleration is the centripetal acceleration.

Another commonly used relationship is:

a = v²/r

where:

• v = linear speed (m s⁻¹)
• r = radius of the circular path (m)

Combining these two equations gives:

F = mv²/r

Since the centripetal acceleration has already been given in this question, using F = ma is the quickest and most appropriate approach.
explanation.related_formula_connections:
Students should recognise the relationship between the major circular motion formulas:

• Angular velocity: ω = θ/t
• Linear speed: v = ωr
• Centripetal acceleration: a = v²/r
• Centripetal force: F = ma = mv²/r

Knowing how these formulas are connected helps you choose the most suitable equation based on the information provided.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known values.

Mass, m = 3.0 kg
Centripetal acceleration, a = 15 m s⁻²

Step 2: Identify the required quantity.

Required: Centripetal force, F.

Step 3: Select the appropriate formula.

Use Newton's Second Law:

F = ma

Step 4: Substitute the known values.

F = 3.0 × 15

Step 5: Perform the calculation.

F = 45 N

Step 6: Compare with the options.

45 N corresponds to Option C.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (12.5 N) is incorrect because it does not equal the product of the given mass and acceleration.
Option B (18 N) is incorrect because it results from an incorrect multiplication or the use of an inappropriate formula.
Option D (54 N) is incorrect because it overestimates the force and does not satisfy F = ma for the given values.
explanation.misconception_alert: A common mistake is to ignore the given centripetal acceleration and unnecessarily calculate speed first. Another frequent error is to substitute the radius into the equation F = ma, even though it is not required once the acceleration is already known.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever a question provides the centripetal acceleration directly, remember that Newton's Second Law can be applied immediately. This often saves time during the examination and reduces unnecessary calculations.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine pushing a child on a merry-go-round so that the child continues moving in a circle. The harder the child must turn towards the centre, the greater the inward force required. Since the block has a mass of 3.0 kg and experiences an inward acceleration of 15 m s⁻², multiplying them gives the required inward force of 45 N.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Circular Motion → Centripetal Force → Newton's Second Law in Circular Motion for a complete lesson on centripetal acceleration, centripetal force, formula derivations, worked examples and authentic JAMB-style practice questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for beginner-friendly concept explanations and revision exercises.

subtopic: Relationship between centripetal force and speed
question: A tricycle (Keke) negotiates a circular market junction of radius 18 m at a speed of 6 m s⁻¹. To reduce travel time, the rider later increases the speed to 12 m s⁻¹ while following the same circular path. Assuming the mass of the tricycle and the radius remain unchanged, how does the required centripetal force change?

options.B: It becomes three times the original value.
options.C: It becomes four times the original value.

answer_text: It becomes four times the original value.

explanation.question_restate: This question examines how changing the speed of an object moving in a circle affects the centripetal force required to keep it on the same circular path.
explanation.principle:
Before solving this question, it is important to understand what centripetal force means.

Whenever an object moves in a circular path, its direction changes continuously. Even if its speed remains constant, its velocity changes because velocity has both magnitude and direction. A change in velocity means the object is accelerating.

This inward acceleration is called centripetal acceleration, and according to Newton's Second Law, every acceleration is produced by a force. The inward force responsible for maintaining circular motion is called centripetal force.

For a vehicle negotiating a bend, the tyres and the road provide this inward force through friction. If the available centripetal force is insufficient, the vehicle may skid out of the bend.
explanation.foundational_knowledge:
The fundamental formula for centripetal force is:

F = mv²/r

where:

F = centripetal force in newtons (N)
m = mass in kilograms (kg)
v = speed in metres per second (m s⁻¹)
r = radius of the circular path in metres (m)

This is a fundamental relationship in circular motion. It shows that centripetal force is directly proportional to the mass and to the square of the speed, but inversely proportional to the radius.

A related formula is the centripetal acceleration:

a = v²/r

Since F = ma, substituting a = v²/r gives F = mv²/r.
steps:
Step 1: Identify what changes.

The speed increases from 6 m s⁻¹ to 12 m s⁻¹.

The mass and the radius remain constant.

Step 2: Apply the proportional relationship.

Since F ∝ v²,

F₂/F₁ = (v₂/v₁)²

Step 3: Substitute the speeds.

F₂/F₁ = (12/6)² = 2² = 4

Step 4: Interpret the result.

The required centripetal force becomes four times the original value.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option C.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because doubling the speed does not merely double the centripetal force. The force depends on the square of the speed.
Option B is incorrect because there is no direct proportionality of three in this situation. Squaring the speed ratio gives four.
Option D is incorrect because increasing the speed while maintaining the same circular path requires a greater inward force.
explanation.misconception_alert: A common mistake is to assume that if the speed doubles, the centripetal force also doubles. Since the speed is squared in the formula, doubling the speed makes the required centripetal force four times greater.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever a JAMB question states that the mass and radius remain unchanged, focus on the relationship F ∝ v². You can often answer such questions without calculating the actual force.
explanation.final_answer: C. It becomes four times the original value.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine swinging a small stone tied to a string. If you swing it twice as fast while keeping the string the same length, you must pull much harder on the string to keep the stone moving in a circle. In fact, the pull becomes four times greater because centripetal force depends on the square of the speed.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Circular Motion → Centripetal Force → Relationship Between Force and Speed for detailed lessons on proportional reasoning, worked examples, and JAMB-style practice questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for beginner-friendly concept explanations and examination-focused revision.

question:
Two cyclists ride at constant speeds on separate circular tracks.

Cyclist P has a mass of 60 kg and moves at 8 m s⁻¹ around a track of radius 20 m.

Cyclist Q has a mass of 72 kg and moves at 10 m s⁻¹ around a track of radius 30 m.

Based on the centripetal forces acting on the two cyclists, which of the following statements is correct?

options.A: Cyclist P experiences the greater centripetal force.
options.B: Cyclist Q experiences the greater centripetal force.
options.C: Both cyclists experience the same centripetal force.
options.D: The centripetal force cannot be compared because the masses are different.

answer_text: Cyclist Q experiences the greater centripetal force.

explanation.question_restate: This question compares the centripetal forces acting on two cyclists travelling in uniform circular motion on different tracks. You are required to calculate the force acting on each cyclist before deciding which is greater.
explanation.principle:
Whenever an object moves round a circular path, its direction changes continuously even if its speed remains constant. This continuous change in direction means the object is accelerating towards the centre of the circle. The inward acceleration is called centripetal acceleration, and the force responsible for producing it is called centripetal force.

A beginner may ask why a cyclist needs an inward force. Without this inward force, the cyclist would move in a straight line due to inertia instead of following the curved path.

The fundamental formula for centripetal force is:

F = mv²/r

where:
• F = centripetal force in newtons (N)
• m = mass in kilograms (kg)
• v = linear speed in metres per second (m s⁻¹)
• r = radius of the circular path in metres (m)

This formula is derived from Newton's Second Law, F = ma, together with the centripetal acceleration formula, a = v²/r.

Related formulas include:
• a = v²/r for centripetal acceleration.
• v = ωr when angular velocity is known.
• F = mω²r when angular velocity is used instead of linear speed.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known values.

Cyclist P:
m = 60 kg
v = 8 m s⁻¹
r = 20 m

Cyclist Q:
m = 72 kg
v = 10 m s⁻¹
r = 30 m.

Step 2: Calculate the centripetal force on Cyclist P.

F = mv²/r
= (60 × 8²)/20
= (60 × 64)/20
= 3840/20
= 192 N.

Step 3: Calculate the centripetal force on Cyclist Q.

F = mv²/r
= (72 × 10²)/30
= (72 × 100)/30
= 7200/30
= 240 N.

Step 4: Compare the results.

Cyclist P: 192 N
Cyclist Q: 240 N

Since 240 N is greater than 192 N, Cyclist Q experiences the greater centripetal force.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option B.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because Cyclist P experiences only 192 N, which is less than 240 N.
Option C is incorrect because the calculated centripetal forces are not equal.
Option D is incorrect because centripetal forces can be compared directly by calculating them using the formula F = mv²/r.
explanation.misconception_alert: A common mistake is to compare only the speeds or only the masses. Centripetal force depends on three quantities simultaneously: mass, the square of the speed, and the radius of the circular path. All three must be considered before drawing a conclusion.
explanation.exam_tip: When comparing centripetal forces, calculate the force for each object separately using F = mv²/r. Remember that speed has the greatest influence because it is squared. A small increase in speed can produce a much larger increase in centripetal force.
explanation.final_answer: B. Cyclist Q experiences the greater centripetal force.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine two children riding bicycles around different playground tracks.
